title: support for rootless containers with an external rootfs
body:
This is a hacky and PoC attempt at having rootless containers in podman.  A proper implementation will take much more work than this PR (biggest issue, containers/storage must handle rootless access and management for the images, most likely we will need some changes in containers/image as well).  In any case we would be able to use only the vfs backend storage until overlayfs can be used by a not privileged user.

Given these limitations, the current implementation expects an exploded container rootfs.  My tests were limited to run a container and see it appears in `podman ps`.

The last patch must land in containers/storage, I've added it here so that the PR can be used.

```console
$ bin/podman run -v /tmp:/tmp --rootfs /path/to/an/exploded/container/rootfs sh -c "echo it works > /tmp/out"; cat /tmp/out
it works
$ bin/podman run -v /tmp:/tmp --rootfs /path/to/an/exploded/container/rootfs cat /proc/self/uid_map
         0       1000          1
```

comments.body: @nalind @mtrmac PTAL
@nalind could you have a look at the patch "[VENDOR-FIX]: containers/storage do not chown if not root".  Does it look fine for containers/storage?
Rebase please
I've simplified the changes in containers/storage, adding a dummy driver that can be used with `--rootfs`.

> I've simplified the changes in containers/storage, adding a dummy driver that can be used with `--rootfs`.

That’s _very_ nice.

@mheon @mtrmac I understand your concern. but you guys are making what I believe is the classic engineering mistake and making the UI more complicated so your job is easier.  
If we enforced full path for the image, that would be better then adding a --rootfs flag.  

But if the code did a simple check to see if the path exists on disk before doing any image processing, I don't see that as being all that complicated.

Yes there will be corner cases when I could have a fedora directory in the current working directory, but then just make the user specify full path.

> but you guys are making what I believe is the classic engineering mistake and making the UI more complicated so your job is easier.

It’s not obvious to me that the `--rootfs` option is less code, and that was not really a concern to me at all.

The way I think about this, UI complexity is not as much a matter of number of options, or even individual characters to type, as a matter of number of _concepts_ and conditions the _user_ needs to keep track of.

In one sense, (`--rootfs explicitA` vs. `explicitB`) and (`guessingA` vs. `guessingB`) are conceptually both two options, so a “similar cognitive load”.  But as far as what the user _must_ understand, `--rootfs` one is much easier to Google for, and clearly a separate alternative; it’s _safe_ for the user to learn about `explicitB` without knowing about `--rootfs explicitA` at all. With `guessingA` vs. `guessingB`, the user _must know both_, and never forget (when, in practice, many will be copy&pasting commands without being aware of the two formats at all).
 
> But if the code did a simple check to see if the path exists on disk before doing any image processing, I don't see that as being all that complicated.
>
> Yes there will be corner cases when I could have a fedora directory in the current working directory, but then just make the user specify full path.

That’s not making the corner case situation any better: the user writes a script that works _just fine_ with for months with one of the semantics, maybe blissfully unaware of the other feature at all, and suddenly the script stops working just because someone (possibly a different person) created an entirely unrelated object on the same machine.
:umbrella: The latest upstream changes (presumably c9f7634) made this pull request unmergeable. Please resolve the merge conflicts.
opened a PR for containers/storage: https://github.com/containers/storage/pull/187 to add the new driver
Question: why not just use `vfs` driver (in `unshare -U -m` environment)?

Hi @AkihiroSuda.  That would work as well, but I wanted to avoid the overhead of setting another user namespace.  Since we want to support multiple UIDs/GIDs we would need to take care of that as well (with newuidmap/newgidmap).  I'd like in the long term to teach containers/storage how to run as non root and avoid privileged operations like Chown
@giuseppe Can I Chown within a UserNS TO my range of UIDS?
@rhatdan yes, within an userNS (where the user has CAP_CHOWN)  it is possible to use Chown to any user mapped in the namespace.

After talking to @nalind about this, I think we could do better and just use the VFS layer.
Syntax should be standard.

podman run -ti fedora sh

And podman figure out that it is running in UID!=0. 
This changes storage to be ~/containers
Change Driver to be vfs
The podman process changes to the UserNS immediately with UserMapping  specified in /etc/subuid. Then pulls the image if it does not exist.  And launches the runc container.  

I think this could be made to work, and would be a tremendous new capability.

@baude @mheon @rhatdan I addressed the comments above.  If there are no other objections, let's merge this as it is already carrying a substantial amount of changes.  I'll start working on creating an userNS for handling containers/storage on top of these changes.

@giuseppe I do not like the way the CLI works for this.  I would rather stick to images and not deal with rootfs at all.  Why can't we leave this the standard CLI and have podman be smart enough to pull images to ~/containers/storage vfs layer and just use them there.  Then this is little no change in how containers/storage works and the CLI is the same whether  you are running as root or not as root.

I think that the `--rootfs` feature is not only useful for the rootless case but that in general having a way to specify an exploded rootfs that is not managed by containers/storage will enable some new use cases (especially with a read only rootfs) e.g. 1) the same rootfs can be used by multiple containers 2) the rootfs could come from an NFS share.

> ... the rootfs could come from an NFS share.

Or from IPFS :).  Digging *way* back into my archives, here's a pre-OCI example: ipfs-shipyard/container-demos#22.  Personally I'm fine with or without a `--rootfs` option, but having a way to launch containers from exploded directories is great, because it allows folks to easily plug in alternative image-distribution stacks.

I'd like to hold off on merging for a few days. We don't have anything in the pipeline to majorly change things this touches and introduce merge conflicts, and I'd like to do more testing. Image-based containers was a pretty fundamental assumption in libpod until now, I'd like to poke around and see if we broke anything by removing it.

these are the results I've got:

`podman images`: doesn't show anything different as there is not any new image
`podman ps`: shows correctly the new container
`podman mount`: returns the path to the rootfs
`podman rm`: didn't cleanup the shm mount correctly when used as root. It works fine with the new version pushed
`podman top`: works
`podman exec`: works

Just a few more.

podman commit?
podman diff?
podman umount
podman inspect

I've pushed another version with two small changes for `commit` and `inspect`.

`podman commit`: changed to return an error, as we cannot commit the rootfs as it is.
`podman diff`: returns no changes
`podman umount`: exit with success does without touching the rootfs.
`podman inspect`: works as usual, the Image field is empty.  I've added another field `Rootfs`

@giuseppe Is the rootfs treated as readonly?  SELinux must be disabled, I would guess or did you change the label of the content of the rootfs?

I hope to play with this today.
@rhatdan no, but I don't think we should enforce it to be readonly.   A rootfs that is not shared by multiple containers should be usable as rw
@giuseppe Lots of travis failures?

I think we should check 'podman export' as well. That's probably the last
major command that might be affected.

On Mon, Jun 11, 2018, 06:45 Daniel J Walsh <notifications@github.com> wrote:

> @giuseppe <https://github.com/giuseppe> Lots of travis failures?
>
> —
> You are receiving this because you were mentioned.
> Reply to this email directly, view it on GitHub
> <https://github.com/projectatomic/libpod/pull/871#issuecomment-396230761>,
> or mute the thread
> <https://github.com/notifications/unsubscribe-auth/AHYHCG91MFyW235n1jZTOvqrL8JPnJ8Eks5t7maBgaJpZM4UWj5S>
> .
>
bot, retest this please
@mheon `podman export` exports correctly the specified rootfs for the container

(Keep in mind I am only a half-informed outsider to the project; this may be complete nonsense.)

> I think that the --rootfs feature is not only useful for the rootless case but that in general having a way to specify an exploded rootfs that is not managed by containers/storage will enable some new use cases (especially with a read only rootfs) 

That’s basically the `runc` level of abstraction, though, isn’t it? Owning _and managing_ the resources is more or less the point of `podman` / CRI-O.

(I’m not saying that there must be exactly two levels of obstraction, and it is of course quite possible to have a non-default option which “turns off” some of the podman features. It just looks very non-obvious to me how all this works together, and how to explain an intuition behind it to new users.  (E.g. if `podman ps` shows the container for the invoking unprivileged user, does it show it when non-invoking `root` runs it? Should it?))

@mtrmac Well I think each podman instance would be per user.  So one podman will not see the other users using podman or the roots podman.  From an SELinux point of view this could be dangerous since the SELinux labels have a chance of overlapping, but this is something we will have to think about in the future.

> Well I think each podman instance would be per user. So one podman will not see the other users using podman or the roots podman.

OK; in that case I’d expect each of the per-user podman instances to manage its own storage, and the `--rootfs` bypass-storage-management option would’t be commonly used, if it existed at all.

> @giuseppe Lots of travis failures?

the travis failures were caused by a patch that updated the version of conmon used.  Since it is not needed for this PR, I've dropped it and take a look at that separately.

Yes --rootfs bypasses image management in containers storage.
@giuseppe can I use / for my rootfs?

@rhatdan not directly, the OCI runtime won't work with that.  A bind mount should be fine though:

```console
# mount --bind --make-slave / /tmp/root
# podman run --rm --rootfs /tmp/root echo hi
hi
```

I've done more tests with `--rootfs` used with root and everything seems to work fine here.

Differently, the support for rootless is still incomplete, I am still working on managing the vfs storage backend with an userNS.

I've added two more patches.  Podman setups the user namespace that it is used both for the management of the storage and for the OCI container.

runc needs this patch to work: https://github.com/opencontainers/runc/pull/1816

All commands should work, but I've done only some basic testing so far.
@giuseppe Are those patches necessary for the --rootfs support?  Or are they necessary for full image support?
@rhatdan full image support on vfs
@giuseppe Could you open a separate PR, so we can get --rootless merged and then we can look at your image stuff separatly.  This PR set is getting large now.  Need @mheon for review of it.

@rhatdan I've moved the new patches to https://github.com/projectatomic/libpod/pull/936.

It is based on this PR, I'll rebase once this gets merged
thanks for the review.  I've addressed the comments and pushed a new version :arrow_up:
if there are no other blockers, let's merge this first part so that I can continue on: https://github.com/projectatomic/libpod/pull/936
@giuseppe I'll give this a last review pass first thing tomorrow and merge once that's done
@cyphar sure.  Would it make sense to have `umoci` as a backend in containers/storage so that it can be used by every other tool already using `container/storage`?

@giuseppe Yes that would be the idea. I would need to have a quick chat with @nalind about how he would like me to fit `umoci` into that use-case, but I definitely like that idea. One other unrelated point is that I think the OCI code in `containers/image` should just use `umoci`'s OCI parsing and handling code (but I'll talk to @mtrmac about that separately).
@cyphar If you can get umoci into containers/storage I would be all for it.

@giuseppe I tried to build new packages for runc, but the current runc is not building on i686.

```
go build -buildmode=pie  -ldflags "-X main.gitCommit="" -X main.version=1.0.0-rc5+dev " -tags "seccomp selinux" -o runc .
# github.com/opencontainers/runc/libcontainer/system
GOPATH/src/github.com/opencontainers/runc/libcontainer/system/linux.go:119:89: constant 4294967295 overflows int
make: *** [Makefile:32: runc] Error 2
error: Bad exit status from /var/tmp/rpm-tmp.cx5NQN (%build)
    Bad exit status from /var/tmp/rpm-tmp.cx5NQN (%build)

````
I don't have time to look into this, but I think we need a fix in runc to work on 32 bit systems.

https://kojipkgs.fedoraproject.org//work/tasks/1255/27631255/build.log
:umbrella: The latest upstream changes (presumably 4b4de5d) made this pull request unmergeable. Please resolve the merge conflicts.

@giuseppe I'm a little iffy on merging this without a bit more documentation on `--rootfs` -- it automatically configured a separate store and database if you're not UID 0, so you can't see containers created with it with `podman ps` from root. This deserves some sort of change to the manpages, I think?

Otherwise, LGTM.

@mheon this PR is needed mostly in preparation of #936.  If you are fine with it, I can add more documentation about rootless containers there as rootless containers are not really usable yet without the changes in containers/storage for skipping Chown's.  The alternative solution of using an userNS, and that really enables this user case, is implemented in the other PR
@giuseppe Alright. We still needs manpages + bash completions for `--rootfs` in this PR, though.
@mheon sure, I've added `--rootfs` to the man page and the bash autocompletion
bot, retest this please
Tests are green, code LGTM.
@rh-atomic-bot r+

:pushpin: Commit 4932a89 has been approved by `mheon`

<!-- @rh-atomic-bot r=mheon 4932a89be57a63c0ea69faa08313148aa688a371 -->
:zap: Test exempted: pull fully rebased and already tested.

> One other unrelated point is that I think the OCI code in containers/image should just use umoci's OCI parsing and handling code (but I'll talk to @mtrmac about that separately).

(Sounds good: containers/image/oci , and the WIP work on OCI multi-arch, handles only a small subset of the possible OCI layouts, to keep the code manageable at the possible cost of interoperability. If we can’t have a simple OCI format, standardizing on a single implementation of the lookup/multi-image/… semantics is the next best way to get widespread interoperability.)

Can someone enlighten me as to what an "exploded container rootfs" is? Assuming I have ran ```debootstrap``` and have a simple rootfs, what am I to do?

```
➜ podman run --rootfs ./rootfs /bin/bash
Error: rootfs (/home/pknopf/.local/share/containers/storage/vfs-containers/ca37fcd22cedf112c349183200e28040d93b7d299cded3a4d9d2f68129885583/userdata/rootfs) does not exist: OCI runtime error
➜ ls rootfs
bin   dev  home  lib64  mnt  proc  run   srv  tmp  var
boot  etc  lib   media  opt  root  sbin  sys  usr
```

Digging into the generated ```config.json```, it looks like the relative path ```./rootfs``` isn't expanded. Using an absolutely path to ```--rootfs``` worked. It might be worth auto expanding relative paths in the future.
Please open a PR to do this? or at least open a separate issue.
